message.abstract:
<jats:p>
            To determine whether clinical outcomes differed by occurrence of severe hypertension in the international CHIPS trial (Control of Hypertension in Pregnancy Study), adjusting for the interventions of “less tight” (target diastolic blood pressure [dBP] 100 mm Hg) versus “tight” control (target dBP 85 mm Hg). In this post-hoc analysis of CHIPS data from 987 women with nonsevere nonproteinuric preexisting or gestational hypertension, mixed effects logistic regression was used to compare the following outcomes according to occurrence of severe hypertension, adjusting for allocated group and the influence of baseline factors: CHIPS primary (perinatal loss or high-level neonatal care for >48 hours) and secondary outcomes (serious maternal complications), birth weight <10th percentile, preeclampsia, delivery at <34 or <37 weeks, platelets <100×10
            <jats:sup>9</jats:sup>
            /L, elevated liver enzymes with symptoms, maternal length of stay ≥10 days, and maternal readmission before 6 weeks postpartum. Three hundred and thirty-four (34.1%) women in CHIPS developed severe hypertension that was associated with all outcomes examined except for maternal readmission (
            <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
            =0.20): CHIPS primary outcome, birth weight <10th percentile, preeclampsia, preterm delivery, elevated liver enzymes (all
            <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
            <0.001), platelets <100×10
            <jats:sup>9</jats:sup>
            /L (
            <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
            =0.006), and prolonged hospital stay (
            <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
            =0.03). The association between severe hypertension and serious maternal complications was seen only in less tight control (
            <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
            =0.02). Adjustment for preeclampsia (464, 47.3%) did not negate the relationship between severe hypertension and the CHIPS primary outcome (
            <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
            <0.001), birth weight <10th percentile (
            <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
            =0.005), delivery at <37 (
            <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
            <0.001) or <34 weeks (
            <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
            <0.001), or elevated liver enzymes with symptoms (
            <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
            =0.02). Severe hypertension is a risk marker for adverse maternal and perinatal outcomes, independent of BP control or preeclampsia co-occurrence.
          </jats:p>
          <jats:sec>
            <jats:title>Clinical Trial Registration—</jats:title>
            <jats:p>
              URL:
              <jats:ext-link xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" ext-link-type="uri" xlink:href="http://pre-empt.cfri.ca/">http://pre-empt.cfri.ca/</jats:ext-link>
              . Unique identifier: ISRCTN 71416914. URL:
              <jats:ext-link xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" ext-link-type="uri" xlink:href="https://www.clinicaltrials.gov/">https://www.clinicaltrials.gov/</jats:ext-link>
              . Unique identifier: NCT01192412.
            </jats:p>
          </jats:sec>

reference.article-title: Antihypertensive drug therapy for mild to moderate hypertension during pregnancy.
reference.author: Abalos E
reference.unstructured: Abalos E, Duley L, Steyn DW. Antihypertensive drug therapy for mild to moderate hypertension during pregnancy. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2014;2:CD002252.

reference.article-title: Pregnancy outcomes in healthy nulliparas who developed hypertension. Calcium for Preeclampsia Prevention Study Group.
reference.author: Hauth JC
reference.unstructured: Hauth JC, Ewell MG, Levine RJ, Esterlitz JR, Sibai B, Curet LB, Catalano PM, Morris CD. Pregnancy outcomes in healthy nulliparas who developed hypertension. Calcium for Preeclampsia Prevention Study Group. Obstet Gynecol. 2000;95:24–28.

subtitle: Is Severe Hypertension Just an Elevated Blood Pressure?
title: The CHIPS Randomized Controlled Trial (Control of Hypertension in Pregnancy Study)
